3.4 Bases>pOH and relationship between pH and pOH
3.4 Bases>Common uses of NaOH and Ca(OH)2
3.5 Salts>General Methods for the Preparation of Salts
3.5 Salts>Chemical Tests of Some Ions in Salt

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.subsection: pOH and relationship between pH and pOH
questions.question: At \(25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\), what is the sum \(\mathrm{pH} + \mathrm{pOH}\) for any aqueous solution?
questions.hint: Recall the ion product constant of water.
questions.explanation: The sum \(\mathrm{pH} + \mathrm{pOH}\) always equals 14 at \(25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) because \(\mathrm{Kw} = [\mathrm{H^+}][\mathrm{OH^-}] = 1.0 \times 10^{-14}\). Taking negative logarithms gives \(\mathrm{pH} + \mathrm{pOH} = 14\).

source_trace.subsection_anchor: pOH and relationship between pH and pOH

explanations_tiered.definition: The sum pH + pOH equals 14 at 25°C due to the ion product constant of water (Kw = 1.0 × 10⁻¹⁴).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Taking negative logarithms of Kw = [H⁺][OH⁻] = 10⁻¹⁴ gives pH + pOH = 14, valid for all aqueous solutions at 25°C.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The pH and pOH of any water-based solution always add up to 14 at room temperature — like opposite ends of a see-saw.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: From Kw = [H⁺][OH⁻] = 1×10⁻¹⁴, applying -log gives (-log[H⁺]) + (-log[OH⁻]) = 14, hence pH + pOH = 14.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Kw varies with temperature (e.g., at 50°C, Kw ≈ 5.5×10⁻¹⁴, so pH + pOH ≈ 13.26). At 25°C, the sum is exactly 14.
worked_solution: Step 1: Recall Kw = [H⁺][OH⁻] = 1.0×10⁻¹⁴ at 25°C.
Step 2: Take -log of both sides: -log([H⁺][OH⁻]) = -log(1.0×10⁻¹⁴).
Step 3: -log[H⁺] + (-log[OH⁻]) = 14.
Step 4: Therefore pH + pOH = 14.
reasoning_steps: Remember the definition of pH and pOH.
misconceptions: The sum is 14 only at 25°C; at other temperatures it changes because Kw changes.

glossary_entry.definition: The negative logarithm of the hydroxide ion concentration: pOH = -log[OH⁻].

flashcard.front: What is the relationship between pH and pOH at 25°C?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: At 25°C, if a solution has pH = 5, what is its pOH?
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Stress that this relationship holds only at 25°C.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which reaction is used to prepare sodium hydroxide (\(\mathrm{NaOH}\)) from sodium metal?
questions.hint: Reactive metals react vigorously with water.
questions.explanation: Sodium metal reacts violently with water to produce sodium hydroxide and hydrogen gas: \(\mathrm{2Na + 2H_2O \rightarrow 2NaOH + H_2}\). Option B is also a valid preparation method (metal oxide + water), but the question specifies from sodium metal.

source_trace.content_anchor: highly reactive metals from Group IA and Group IIA with water ... 2Na + 2H2O -> 2NaOH + H2

explanations_tiered.definition: Reactive metals react with water to produce metal hydroxides and hydrogen gas.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Group I metals (Li, Na, K) react exothermically with cold water to form the metal hydroxide and H₂.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Sodium is so reactive that it fizzes violently in water, producing sodium hydroxide (a base) and hydrogen gas bubbles.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Equation: \(\mathrm{2Na(s) + 2H_2O(l) \rightarrow 2NaOH(aq) + H_2(g)}\). The solution becomes alkaline due to NaOH.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The reaction is a redox: Na is oxidized (0 to +1), H₂O is reduced (H⁺ to H₂). This method is too violent for laboratory preparation; usually NaOH is made by electrolysis of NaCl.
worked_solution: Step 1: Na is a Group 1 metal.
Step 2: Group 1 metals react with water: 2M + 2H₂O → 2MOH + H₂.
Step 3: For Na: 2Na + 2H₂O → 2NaOH + H₂.
reasoning_steps: Recall that active metals (Group 1) react with water to form bases.
Write the balanced equation for Na + H₂O.
misconceptions: Na + H₂O does not produce Na₂O; it directly produces NaOH and H₂ gas.

glossary_entry.definition: Reaction of an active metal with water producing a metal hydroxide and hydrogen gas.

flashcard.front: What products are formed when sodium reacts with cold water?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How is NaOH prepared from sodium metal?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Active metal + water → metal hydroxide + H₂.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: DO NOT demo sodium in water — extremely dangerous. Use video demonstration.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Sodium reacts with water to produce NaOH and H₂.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.subsection: Common uses of NaOH and Ca(OH)2
questions.question: Which substance is commonly used to neutralize acidic soil?
questions.hint: Farmers add slaked lime to fields.
questions.explanation: Calcium hydroxide (\(\mathrm{Ca(OH)_2}\), slaked lime) is cheap, non-corrosive, and widely used in agriculture to neutralize acidic soils. NaOH and KOH are too caustic and expensive for field use.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: Common uses of NaOH and Ca(OH)2
source_trace.content_anchor: Calcium hydroxide is usually added to neutralize acidic soil

explanations_tiered.definition: Calcium hydroxide (slaked lime) is used to neutralize soil acidity because it is inexpensive, safe to handle, and effective.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Agricultural lime (Ca(OH)₂) reacts with soil H⁺ to raise pH. It is preferred over NaOH or KOH due to low cost and lower corrosiveness.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Farmers spread lime (calcium hydroxide) on fields to make soil less sour (less acidic) so crops grow better.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Ca(OH)₂ neutralizes acidic soil: \(\mathrm{Ca(OH)_2 + 2H^+ \rightarrow Ca^{2+} + 2H_2O}\). It is safer and cheaper than caustic soda.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Liming also provides Ca²⁺, an essential plant nutrient, and improves soil structure. Over-liming can cause micronutrient deficiencies.
reasoning_steps: Recall common agricultural base: slaked lime.
Identify Ca(OH)₂ as the correct choice.
misconceptions: NaOH would neutralize soil but is too dangerous and expensive; Ca(OH)₂ is the practical choice.

glossary_entry.definition: Calcium hydroxide, used to neutralize acidic soil and in cement.

flashcard.front: What is added to acidic soil to neutralize it?
flashcard.back: Calcium hydroxide (\(\mathrm{Ca(OH)_2}\)), also called slaked lime.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Why is Ca(OH)₂ preferred over NaOH for soil treatment?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: It is cheaper, less caustic, and also provides calcium as a plant nutrient.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Ca(OH)₂ used in agriculture and construction.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Ca(OH)₂ is widely used to neutralize acidic soil.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: What term describes a salt that absorbs water vapor from the atmosphere and becomes a solution?
questions.hint: The salt dissolves in the water it absorbs.
questions.explanation: Deliquescent salts absorb enough water from the air to form a solution. Examples: \(\mathrm{CaCl_2}\) and \(\mathrm{FeCl_3}\). Hygroscopic salts absorb water but remain solid; efflorescent salts lose water.

source_trace.content_anchor: Deliquescent salts absorb water from the atmosphere to form a solution

explanations_tiered.definition: A deliquescent substance absorbs water vapor from the air until it dissolves completely into a solution.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Deliquescence is different from hygroscopy (absorbs but stays solid) and efflorescence (loses water). Examples: CaCl₂, FeCl₃, NaOH pellets.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Some salts are so thirsty for water that they suck moisture from the air and melt into a puddle — that's deliquescent.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Calcium chloride (CaCl₂) is deliquescent: it absorbs atmospheric moisture and forms a concentrated solution. It is used as a drying agent but also self-dissolves.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Deliquescence occurs when the vapor pressure of the saturated solution is lower than the partial pressure of water vapor in the air. The salt continually absorbs water until it is fully dissolved.
reasoning_steps: Recall definition: deliquescent = absorbs water + dissolves.
Select term that means forms a solution.
misconceptions: Hygroscopic salts also absorb water but do not liquefy; only deliquescent salts become solutions.

glossary_entry.definition: A substance that absorbs moisture from the air and dissolves completely.

flashcard.front: What happens to a deliquescent salt left open to air?
flashcard.back: It absorbs water vapor and eventually dissolves into a solution.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the difference between hygroscopic and deliquescent?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Hygroscopic absorbs water but stays solid; deliquescent absorbs water and dissolves into a solution.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A substance that absorbs moisture and dissolves.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Show CaCl₂ open to air turning into liquid.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Deliquescent salts absorb water and form a solution.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: What products are formed when calcium carbonate (\(\mathrm{CaCO_3}\)) is heated strongly?
questions.hint: Metal carbonates decompose to metal oxides and carbon dioxide.
questions.explanation: Heating \(\mathrm{CaCO_3}\) (limestone) causes thermal decomposition: \(\mathrm{CaCO_3 \rightarrow CaO + CO_2}\). Calcium oxide (quicklime) and carbon dioxide are the products.

explanations_tiered.definition: Thermal decomposition of a metal carbonate produces the metal oxide and carbon dioxide gas.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Group II carbonates (except Be) and transition metal carbonates decompose on heating. CaCO₃ is used industrially to make quicklime (CaO).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Heating limestone (calcium carbonate) breaks it down into quicklime (calcium oxide) and carbon dioxide gas.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Equation: \(\mathrm{CaCO_3(s) \xrightarrow{\Delta} CaO(s) + CO_2(g)}\). CaO is used in cement; CO₂ gas is released.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The decomposition temperature decreases down Group II? Actually, MgCO₃ decomposes at ~350°C, CaCO₃ at ~840°C, BaCO₃ requires ~1360°C. Lattice energy and CO₃²⁻ polarizability affect stability.
worked_solution: Step 1: Identify the reaction type: thermal decomposition of a carbonate.
Step 2: General formula: MCO₃ → MO + CO₂.
Step 3: Apply to CaCO₃: CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂.
reasoning_steps: Recall that heating carbonates gives metal oxide + CO₂.
Identify CaO and CO₂ as the products.
misconceptions: The product is CaO, not Ca or Ca(OH)₂. Ca(OH)₂ forms only if CaO reacts with water.

glossary_entry.definition: Calcium oxide (CaO), produced by heating calcium carbonate.

flashcard.front: What is the industrial product of heating limestone (CaCO₃)?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What does CaCO₃ produce when heated?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Breaking down a compound by heating.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. CaCO₃ decomposes to CaO and CO₂ upon strong heating.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which product is NOT formed when lead(II) nitrate (\(\mathrm{Pb(NO_3)_2}\)) is heated?
questions.hint: Transition metal nitrates decompose to metal oxide, NO₂, and O₂.
questions.explanation: Lead(II) nitrate decomposes to lead(II) oxide, nitrogen dioxide, and oxygen: \(\mathrm{2Pb(NO_3)_2 \rightarrow 2PbO + 4NO_2 + O_2}\). Lead metal is not produced.

explanations_tiered.definition: Heating heavy metal nitrates (Pb, Cu, Mg) produces the metal oxide, nitrogen dioxide (brown gas), and oxygen.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Pb(NO₃)₂ decomposes: \(\mathrm{2Pb(NO_3)_2 \rightarrow 2PbO + 4NO_2 + O_2}\). Pb is not formed; PbO is a yellow solid when hot, white when cool.
explanations_tiered.beginner: When you heat lead nitrate, it breaks apart into lead oxide (a powder), brown nitrogen dioxide gas (fumes), and oxygen gas — no pure lead metal.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Brown fumes of NO₂ indicate a nitrate decomposition. The residue is PbO, not Pb metal. Group 1 nitrates (NaNO₃) give nitrites (NaNO₂), not oxides.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The reaction is a redox: Pb²⁺ stays Pb²⁺ in PbO; N in NO₃⁻ (+5) is reduced to NO₂ (+4) and oxidized to O₂ (0).
worked_solution: Step 1: Recall the decomposition of heavy metal nitrates: 2M(NO₃)₂ → 2MO + 4NO₂ + O₂.
Step 2: For Pb: 2Pb(NO₃)₂ → 2PbO + 4NO₂ + O₂.
Step 3: Pb is not a product.
reasoning_steps: Know that Pb(NO₃)₂ on heating gives PbO, NO₂, O₂.
misconceptions: Some think heating metal nitrates always gives metal; actually, only very unreactive metals (Ag, Au) might give metal; most give oxides.

glossary_entry.definition: Heavy metal nitrates decompose to metal oxide, NO₂, and O₂.

flashcard.front: What color fumes indicate a nitrate decomposition reaction?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the solid residue when Pb(NO₃)₂ is heated?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Heating metal nitrate produces oxide, NO₂, O₂.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Demonstrate brown fumes and yellow residue (PbO hot).
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer D. Pb metal is not a product; PbO is formed instead.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.subsection: Chemical Tests of Some Ions in Salt
questions.question: Which reagent is used to test for the presence of sulfate ions (\(\mathrm{SO_4^{2-}}\)) in a solution?
questions.hint: Sulfates form a white precipitate with barium ions.
questions.explanation: Barium chloride (\(\mathrm{BaCl_2}\)) or barium nitrate is added to a solution acidified with HCl. A white precipitate of \(\mathrm{BaSO_4}\) confirms sulfate ions. AgNO₃ tests for halides.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: Chemical Tests of Some Ions in Salt
source_trace.content_anchor: add BaCl2 or Ba(NO3)2 solution and note if a white precipitate is formed

explanations_tiered.definition: The sulfate ion test uses barium chloride in acidified solution to form a white precipitate of barium sulfate.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: \(\mathrm{Ba^{2+} + SO_4^{2-} \rightarrow BaSO_4(s)}\). The precipitate is insoluble in dilute acids, distinguishing it from carbonates or sulfites.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you add barium chloride to a liquid and a white solid appears that won't dissolve in acid, sulfates are present.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Add dilute HCl (to remove carbonates/sulfites), then BaCl₂. A white precipitate indicates SO₄²⁻. AgNO₃ tests for Cl⁻, Br⁻, I⁻.
explanations_tiered.advanced: BaSO₄ has extremely low Ksp (1.1×10⁻¹⁰), so even low [SO₄²⁻] gives a precipitate. The acid prevents false positives from CO₃²⁻ or SO₃²⁻.
worked_solution: Step 1: Acidify the solution with dilute HCl.
Step 2: Add BaCl₂ or Ba(NO₃)₂ solution.
Step 3: Observe white precipitate = SO₄²⁻ present.
misconceptions: AgNO₃ gives white precipitate with Cl⁻, not SO₄²⁻.

glossary_entry.definition: Adding BaCl₂ to acidified solution produces white BaSO₄ precipitate.

flashcard.front: What indicates a positive sulfate test?
flashcard.back: A white precipitate of barium sulfate (\(\mathrm{BaSO_4}\)).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Why is acid added before testing for sulfates with BaCl₂?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: To remove carbonates and sulfites that could also give white precipitates with Ba²⁺.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: BaCl₂ + acid → white precipitate confirms SO₄²⁻.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Show vs carbonate (fizzes with acid) and sulfate (no fizz, white precipitate).
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. BaCl₂ produces a white precipitate of BaSO₄ in the presence of sulfate ions.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.subsection: Chemical Tests of Some Ions in Salt
questions.question: A salt gives a lilac flame color in a flame test. Which cation is likely present?
questions.explanation: Potassium ions (\(\mathrm{K^+}\)) produce a characteristic lilac (pale violet) flame color. Sodium gives yellow, calcium gives brick red, barium gives apple green.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: Chemical Tests of Some Ions in Salt
source_trace.content_anchor: Flame tests: K+ gives lilac flame

explanations_tiered.definition: A flame test uses a Bunsen burner to identify metal ions by the characteristic color they impart to the flame.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: K⁺ lilac, Na⁺ yellow, Ca²⁺ brick red, Ba²⁺ apple green, Cu²⁺ blue-green, Li⁺ crimson.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Each metal ion burns with a specific color. Potassium burns with a lilac (purple-pink) flame, like a firework.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: A clean nichrome wire is dipped in the salt and held in the Bunsen flame. Lilac flame indicates K⁺. Yellow flame indicates Na⁺, which can mask K⁺ unless viewed through cobalt glass.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Flame color arises from electronic transitions of metal atoms. The energy difference between ground and excited states determines the wavelength of emitted light. K⁺ emits at 766.5 nm and 769.9 nm (violet).
worked_solution: Step 1: Recall flame colors: K = lilac, Na = yellow, Ca = brick red, Ba = apple green.
Step 2: Match lilac to K⁺.
misconceptions: Lilac is sometimes confused with purple; it's a pale violet. Sodium yellow can mask it.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Flame test: K⁺ = lilac; Na⁺ = yellow.

glossary_entry.definition: A qualitative test for metal ions based on flame color.

flashcard.front: What flame color indicates potassium ions?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Why is a cobalt glass filter used when testing for K⁺ in the presence of Na⁺?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Cobalt glass absorbs the yellow sodium light, allowing the lilac potassium color to be seen.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Identifies metal ions by flame color.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Demonstrate flame tests for different metal salts.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. K⁺ gives a lilac flame in a flame test.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.subsection: Chemical Tests of Some Ions in Salt
questions.question: What confirms the presence of carbonate ions (\(\mathrm{CO_3^{2-}}\)) in a solid salt?

options.A: A white precipitate with BaCl₂ solution
options.B: Bubbles of gas that turn limewater milky when acid is added
options.C: A brown ring at the interface of concentrated H₂SO₄

questions.explanation: Adding a dilute acid (e.g., HCl) to a carbonate produces CO₂ gas, which turns limewater (\(\mathrm{Ca(OH)_2}\)) milky due to \(\mathrm{CaCO_3}\) precipitation. Option A tests for sulfates, C for nitrates.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: Chemical Tests of Some Ions in Salt

explanations_tiered.definition: Carbonate ions react with dilute acid to produce carbon dioxide, which turns limewater milky.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The test: add dilute HCl to the solid; effervescence (CO₂) occurs. Pass gas through limewater; a white precipitate confirms CO₃²⁻.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you drop acid on baking soda (carbonate), it fizzes. If you bubble that gas through limewater, the water turns cloudy — that's the carbonate test.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Reactions: \(\mathrm{Na_2CO_3 + 2HCl \rightarrow 2NaCl + CO_2 + H_2O}\); \(\mathrm{CO_2 + Ca(OH)_2 \rightarrow CaCO_3(s) + H_2O}\). The milky white precipitate is calcium carbonate.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The limewater test is specific for CO₂. Bicarbonates (HCO₃⁻) also produce CO₂ with acid, so the test cannot distinguish between CO₃²⁻ and HCO₃⁻ without further quantitative analysis.
worked_solution: Step 1: Add dilute HCl to sample. If bubbles (CO₂) appear, proceed.
Step 2: Bubble gas through limewater (Ca(OH)₂ solution).
Step 3: White precipitate (CaCO₃) = CO₃²⁻ confirmed.
reasoning_steps: Recall carbonate test: acid + carbonate → CO₂.
misconceptions: Other gases (SO₂) can also turn limewater milky temporarily? SO₂ forms CaSO₃, which is white, but SO₂ is acidic and smell; carbonates produce CO₂ only.

glossary_entry.definition: A saturated solution of Ca(OH)₂ used to test for carbon dioxide.

flashcard.front: How do you test for carbonate ions in a solid?
flashcard.back: Add dilute acid and bubble the gas through limewater; a milky white precipitate confirms CO₃²⁻.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What visual change occurs in limewater during a positive carbonate test?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: It turns milky due to formation of CaCO₃ precipitate.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Demonstrate with CaCO₃ + HCl and limewater.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. CO₂ from carbonate turns limewater milky.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which element is classified as a primary macronutrient for plants?
questions.explanation: Nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium (NPK) are the primary macronutrients required in large amounts by plants. Iron, zinc, and boron are micronutrients (trace elements).

source_trace.content_anchor: Nitrogen, phosphorus and potassium are said to be primary mineral nutrients

explanations_tiered.definition: Primary macronutrients are nitrogen (N), phosphorus (P), and potassium (K) — the NPK elements required in large quantities by plants.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Macronutrients are needed in large amounts; micronutrients in trace amounts. NPK are the most important because they are often deficient in soils.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Plants need big helpings of nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium — like a balanced diet — to grow well. Iron and zinc are needed only in tiny amounts.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: N is for proteins and chlorophyll, P for ATP and DNA, K for enzyme function and water balance. Fe, Zn, B are micronutrients (trace).
explanations_tiered.advanced: N is absorbed as NO₃⁻ or NH₄⁺, P as H₂PO₄⁻, K as K⁺. Primary deficiency symptoms: N causes yellowing, P causes purpling, K causes scorching of leaf edges.
reasoning_steps: Identify nitrogen as one of them.
misconceptions: Iron is an important nutrient but is a micronutrient, not a primary macronutrient.

glossary_entry.definition: Nitrogen, phosphorus, or potassium — elements required in large amounts for plant growth.

flashcard.front: What does NPK stand for in fertilizers?
flashcard.back: Nitrogen (N), Phosphorus (P), Potassium (K) — the primary macronutrients.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Name the three primary macronutrients for plants.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Nitrogen is a primary macronutrient; Fe, Zn, B are micronutrients.
